entry_description: A disruption of the ability of other users or processes to obtain timely and reliable access to an AI system's outputs or functionality (availability breakdown).
register_names: Residual AI system security and availability weaknesses
note: Availability breakdown of the AI system is the register's general AI security weakness and availability-attack risk; denial of service and cost-harvesting are the ATLAS-anchored sub-risks.

entry_description: An AI system being forced to misperform against its intended objectives, producing outputs or predictions that align with the attacker's objective.
register_names: Adversarial examples and evasion attacks | Data and model poisoning and backdoors | Prompt injection and jailbreaking
note: Integrity violations are realised through evasion (MR-012), poisoning and backdoors (MR-014) and prompt injection (MR-010); the ATLAS integrity-erosion and trusted-output-manipulation sub-risks apply.

entry_description: The unauthorized access of restricted or proprietary information that is part of an AI system, including information about a model's training data, weights or architecture, or sensitive information that the model accesses (e.g. a RAG knowledge base).
register_names: Privacy-invasive inference and re-identification | Leakage of personal or sensitive data | Model theft, extraction and weight leakage
note: Privacy compromise spans privacy-invasive inference (MR-017), leakage of personal or sensitive data (MR-009) and model or data extraction (MR-016).

entry_description: Misuse enablement: a circumvention of technical restrictions imposed by the AI system's owner on its use, such as restrictions designed to prevent a GenAI system from producing outputs that could cause harm to others.
register_names: Deliberate misuse and repurposing for harm | Prompt injection and jailbreaking
note: Misuse enablement (circumventing owner-imposed safety restrictions, e.g. jailbreaks) maps to misuse or repurposing (MR-032) achieved via guardrail bypass (MR-010.2).

entry_description: Attacks that compromise components introduced through the AI supply chain, such as poisoned third-party data or a poisoned model published to a repository.
register_names: AI supply-chain and infrastructure vulnerabilities | Data and model poisoning and backdoors
note: Supply-chain attacks that introduce poisoned data or models map to the AI supply-chain risk (MR-018) and to data and model poisoning (MR-014).

entry_description: A poisoning attack which operates through model control (the attacker can modify the trained model parameters), here aimed at degrading availability.
register_names: Data and model poisoning and backdoors
note: Model poisoning through model control is direct manipulation or backdoor insertion under data and model poisoning (MR-014).

entry_description: A poisoning attack in which the adversary controls training samples but cannot change their labels (the label-limit capability).
register_names: Data and model poisoning and backdoors
note: Clean-label poisoning is a training-data poisoning variant under MR-014.

entry_description: A poisoning attack in which an adversary controls part of the training data.
register_names: Data and model poisoning and backdoors
note: Data poisoning is squarely MR-014, with ATLAS sub-risks for training-data poisoning and poisoned datasets published for ingestion.

entry_description: An attack that exploits the performance dependency on hardware and model optimizations to negate the effects of hardware optimizations, increase computational latency, increase hardware temperature, and massively increase the amount of energy consumed (e.g. sponge examples).
register_names: Residual AI system security and availability weaknesses
note: Energy-latency (sponge) attacks degrade availability and inflate compute cost, mapping to general AI availability attacks (MR-015), denial of service and denial of wallet.

entry_description: A type of prompt injection executed through resource control (adversary-controlled external data the model ingests) rather than through user-provided input as in a direct prompt injection.
register_names: Prompt injection and jailbreaking
note: Indirect prompt injection via adversary-controlled external data is the register's indirect prompt injection sub-risk (MR-010.9).

entry_description: An attack which exploits the concatenation of untrusted input with a prompt constructed by a higher-trust party such as the application designer.
register_names: Prompt injection and jailbreaking
note: Prompt injection is MR-010 directly, with the deployed-LLM prompt injection sub-risk (MR-010.1).

entry_description: A backdoor poisoning attack mounted without control over training-data labels (a clean-label variant of backdoor poisoning).
register_names: Data and model poisoning and backdoors
note: Clean-label backdoor is a backdoor-insertion variant under data and model poisoning (MR-014).

entry_description: Modifying test samples to create adversarial examples that the model misclassifies at inference time, without altering the model.
register_names: Adversarial examples and evasion attacks
note: Evasion via adversarial examples is the register's adversarial examples and evasion risk (MR-012).

entry_description: A poisoning attack that causes a model to perform an adversary-selected behaviour in response to inputs that follow a particular backdoor pattern.
register_names: Data and model poisoning and backdoors
note: Backdoor poisoning maps to MR-014 with the direct model manipulation and backdoor insertion sub-risk.

entry_description: A poisoning attack that changes the prediction on a small number of targeted samples.
register_names: Data and model poisoning and backdoors
note: Targeted poisoning is a training-data poisoning variant under MR-014.

entry_description: An evasion attack mounted with only query access and no knowledge of the model's parameters or architecture (black-box setting).
register_names: Adversarial examples and evasion attacks
note: Black-box evasion is an evasion attack under MR-012 conducted with query access only.

entry_description: A poisoning attack which operates through model control, here aimed at violating integrity (causing targeted misclassification or adversary-selected behaviour).
register_names: Data and model poisoning and backdoors
note: Model poisoning for integrity maps to MR-014 (direct model manipulation and backdoor insertion).

entry_description: Integrity attacks (often via indirect prompt injection) that cause a GenAI system to become untrustworthy and generate content that deviates from benign behaviour to align with the attacker's objectives (e.g. incorrect summaries, attacker-specified content, suppressed sources, hijacked agents).
register_names: Prompt injection and jailbreaking
note: Misaligned outputs are produced via indirect prompt injection that manipulates trusted output (MR-010.4); the agent-hijack variant is MR-071.

entry_description: A type of privacy attack that extracts details of the model architecture and/or parameters.
register_names: Model theft, extraction and weight leakage
note: Model extraction is the register's model theft and extraction risk (MR-016), via the inference API or white-box access.

entry_description: Privacy attacks that reconstruct sensitive data in a model's training data from aggregate information (data reconstruction).
register_names: Leakage of personal or sensitive data | Privacy-invasive inference and re-identification
note: Data reconstruction has no dedicated register risk; it is covered by leakage of sensitive data (MR-009) and privacy-invasive inference (MR-017).

entry_description: A data privacy attack to determine whether a data sample was part of the training set of a machine learning model.
register_names: Privacy-invasive inference and re-identification
note: Membership inference is the register's privacy-invasive inference and re-identification risk (MR-017).

entry_description: A data privacy attack that infers a global property about the training data of a machine learning model.
register_names: Privacy-invasive inference and re-identification
note: Property inference is a privacy-invasive inference attack under MR-017.

entry_description: An attack that tries to divulge the system prompt or other information in the context of a large language model that would normally be hidden from a user.
register_names: Disclosure of confidential or proprietary information
note: Prompt extraction is system-prompt and instruction extraction (MR-013.2) under disclosure of confidential or proprietary information (MR-013).

entry_description: An indirect prompt injection that instructs a model to persuade the end user to reveal information, then exfiltrates it (e.g. by querying an attacker-controlled URL or via markdown image rendering).
register_names: Leakage of personal or sensitive data
note: Leaking information from user interactions is prompt-induced leakage of sensitive data (MR-009.1) driven by indirect prompt injection (MR-010.9).

entry_description: Training-data extraction: the ability of an attacker to extract the training data of a generative model by prompting the model with specific inputs.
register_names: Leakage of personal or sensitive data
note: Training-data extraction surfaces memorized sensitive data, mapping to leakage of personal or sensitive data (MR-009).

entry_description: Extraction of memorized training data or other sensitive context from a generative model through crafted queries (information-extraction attacks).
register_names: Leakage of personal or sensitive data
note: Data extraction of memorized content maps to leakage of personal or sensitive data in outputs (MR-009).

entry_description: Prompt injection attacks that cause a GenAI system to leak or exfiltrate private information from the restricted resources it can access (e.g. an email client forwarding messages to an attacker inbox; querying an attacker-controlled URL with user data).
register_names: Insecure integration with external tools, plugins and APIs | Autonomous agent hijacking and excessive-agency abuse
note: Compromising connected resources uses injection to exfiltrate data from tools the model can reach, mapping to insecure tool/integration (MR-020) and data exfiltration through agent tool invocation (MR-071.6).

entry_description: Publishing a poisoned model (or poisoned data) into the AI supply chain so that downstream users inherit adversary-controlled behaviour.
register_names: AI supply-chain and infrastructure vulnerabilities | Data and model poisoning and backdoors
note: Supply-chain model poisoning maps to the AI supply-chain risk (MR-018), poisoned models published to registries (MR-018.2) and data and model poisoning (MR-014).
